reviews.reasoning: The question asks students to distinguish Defense Evasion techniques from generic system discovery/profiling behaviors in a specific detonation report. The correct answers (A: proxy config deletion, C: PAGE_GUARD guarded memory, F: suspended process + PE header/shellcode injection) are all directly evidenced in the report signatures. Options B, D, E are correctly identified as discovery/profiling rather than evasion. The question is original, technically precise, and well-calibrated for Hard difficulty. No plagiarism detected against benchmark questions.

The question asks students to identify behaviors that provide explicit evidence of EDR/AV or security-control evasion rather than generic discovery. Correct answers A (reading AV registry keys under HKLM\SOFTWARE\TENCENT), D (modifying proxy settings/IE zone config), and F (writing data to remote process + VirtualAllocEx) are all directly evidenced in the report. Options B (OS version queries), C (service creation), and E (LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress) are correctly excluded as generic discovery/execution. The question is original and technically sound.

The question distinguishes Defense Evasion from Discovery in a UPX-packed infostealer report. Correct answers A (UPX packing/high entropy/zero-size section), C (User Activity Based Checks + Time Based Evasion as sandbox evasion), and E (Dynamic API Resolution + Embedded Payloads) are all directly supported by the report signatures. Options B and D are correctly identified as Discovery. The question is original, well-structured, and appropriately calibrated for Medium difficulty.

The question asks whether the sample performs process injection beyond simple in-process DLL loading. The correct answer A (writing data into a remote process) is directly evidenced by the 'Writes data to a remote process' malicious signature. Options B (LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress - in-process), C (self-thread creation - in-process), D (KsecDD - not injection), and E (dropping PE to temp - not injection) are correctly excluded. The question is original and technically precise for Hard difficulty.

The question asks to identify EDR/AV or sandbox/VM evasion behaviors vs. generic discovery. Correct answers B (checking for AV process names like mcshield.exe), C (querying VMware registry keys and vboxguestadditions strings), and E (modifying proxy settings in HKCU\SOFTWARE\MICROSOFT\WINDOWS\CURRENTVERSION\INTERNET SETTINGS) are all directly evidenced in the report. Options A (computer name/installed apps), D (process enumeration), and F (KsecDD) are correctly excluded. The question is original and well-calibrated.

The question asks to identify Defense Evasion techniques vs. Discovery/Persistence in a PECompact2-packed sample. Correct answers A (proxy settings modification), C (PECompact2 packing + high entropy), and E (writing data to remote process + spawned process likely due to injection) are all directly evidenced in the report. Options B (service creation - Persistence) and D (querying installed apps/security software - Discovery) are correctly excluded. The question is original and technically sound.

The question asks to identify Inhibit System Recovery (Impact) behaviors vs. generic discovery. Correct answers A (vssadmin delete shadows), C (wbadmin delete catalog), and E (bcdedit recoveryenabled no) are all directly evidenced by the process command lines in the report. Options B (OS version queries) and D (process enumeration) are correctly excluded as Discovery. The question is original, technically precise, and well-calibrated for Medium difficulty.

The question asks which ATT&CK techniques are most directly supported by the WannaCry-related DLL report. The correct answers C (T1018 Remote System Discovery - evidenced by ARP broadcast scanning) and D (T1071.001 Web Protocols - evidenced by HTTP GET requests to the kill-switch domain) are directly supported. Options A (T1486 - no encryption observed), B (T1021.002 - no SMB admin shares evidence), E (T1105 - no tool transfer evidence), and F (T1490 - no shadow copy deletion) are correctly excluded. The question is original and technically sound.

The question asks to identify Impact/Anti-Recovery behaviors vs. generic discovery/evasion in a Crysis ransomware report. Correct answers A (vssadmin delete shadows - T1490), C (T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact - listed in MITRE ATT&CK mapping and YARA signature match for dharma_ransomware), and E (marks files for deletion, opens with delete access rights) are all directly evidenced. Options B (FindFirstFileW - Discovery), D (OS version queries - Discovery), and F (packing/obfuscation - Defense Evasion) are correctly excluded. The question is original and well-calibrated.

The question asks which ATT&CK techniques related to network behavior are supported by the Excel CVE-2017-0199 report. Correct answers A (T1071.001 - HTTP GET requests evidenced), C (T1132 - Base64/Base62 encoded HTTP requests evidenced), D (T1573 - HTTPS traffic evidenced), and F (T1071.004 - DNS queries to malicious domains evidenced) are all directly supported by the report signatures. Options B (T1486 - no encryption for impact) and E (T1041 - no explicit exfiltration over C2 channel evidence) are correctly excluded. The question is original and technically sound.

The question asks which ATT&CK techniques are explicitly supported by the Tofsee/BrsecmonE report. Correct answers A (T1055 Process Injection - evidenced by 'Writes data to a remote process' and 'Creates a process in suspended mode'), C (T1562.004 Disable or Modify System Firewall - evidenced by netsh.exe modifying firewall rules), and D (T1543.003 Windows Service - evidenced by sc.exe creating xtblsmos service) are all directly supported. Options B (T1486 - no encryption), E (T1070.006 Timestomp - not explicitly evidenced), and F (T1497 - only generic OS version queries) are correctly excluded. The question is original and well-calibrated for Hard difficulty.

The question asks to identify Defense Evasion behaviors vs. Discovery/Execution in a Fragtor sample. Correct answers A (software packing + high-entropy sections - T1027.002, T1027.009) and D (timestomping + hidden window - T1070.006, T1564.003) are directly evidenced in the report. Options B (system version queries - Discovery), C (Windows service creation - Persistence), and E (rpcrt4.dll loading/GetProcAddress - generic execution) are correctly excluded. The question is original and technically sound.

The question asks to identify sandbox/VM evasion behaviors vs. generic system discovery. Correct answers A (CPUID-based anti-VM trick - directly evidenced by 'The input sample contains a known anti-VM trick' malicious signature) and C (RDTSCP timing instructions - evidenced by 'The input sample possibly contains the RDTSCP instruction') are directly supported. Options B, D, E, F are correctly identified as generic discovery. The question is original and technically precise for Medium difficulty.

The question asks to identify Inhibit System Recovery behaviors vs. generic discovery in a HydraCrypt ransomware report. Correct answers A (vssadmin delete shadows), C (wbadmin delete catalog), and E (bcdedit recoveryenabled no) are all directly evidenced by the process command lines. Options B (OS version queries - Discovery) and D (Windows installation language - Discovery) are correctly excluded. The question is original and well-calibrated.

The question asks which ATT&CK techniques are explicitly supported by the Trojan.Regrun/killers report. Correct answers A (T1055 Process Injection - evidenced by 'Writes data to a remote process' and 'Allocates virtual memory in a remote process'), C (T1036.005 Match Legitimate Name or Location - evidenced by 'Creates a fake system process' for WINLOGON.EXE, CSRSS.EXE etc. at non-system paths), E (T1113 Screen Capture - evidenced by CreateCompatibleBitmap API call), and G (T1027 Obfuscated Files or Information - evidenced by Visual Basic code detection) are all directly supported. Options D (T1003 Credential Dumping - not evidenced), F (T1566.001 Spearphishing - not evidenced, T1189 Drive-by Compromise is listed instead), and H (T1486 - not evidenced) are correctly excluded. The question is original and well-calibrated for Medium difficulty.

The question asks which behaviors provide explicit evidence of process injection vs. dynamic library loading. Correct answers B (writes data to remote process + creates thread - directly evidenced by 'Writes data to a remote process' malicious signature and thread creation) and E (multiple MITRE ATT&CK entries for DLL Injection, Process Hollowing, Process Doppelganging under T1055 family - directly evidenced in the MITRE ATT&CK mapping) are both directly supported. Options A (LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress - in-process, not injection), C (CLR/Bcrypt loading - generic), and D (self-thread creation - not cross-process injection) are correctly excluded. The question is original and technically sound.

The question asks which behaviors classify the Remcos sample as a RAT vs. ransomware/infostealer. Correct answers A (keylogging, credential theft, clipboard/cookie theft - evidenced by T1056.001, T1555.003, T1539, T1115 in MITRE mapping), B (process injection techniques including process hollowing and DLL injection - evidenced by T1055.012, T1055.001 and 'Writes data to a remote process'), and D (network C2 using web protocols with symmetric cryptography - evidenced by T1071.001, T1573.001 and network contacts) are all directly supported. Options C (ransomware encryption - not evidenced) and E (one-time theft with self-deletion - not evidenced, persistent C2 is present) are correctly excluded. The question is original and well-calibrated.

The question asks which network-related ATT&CK techniques are supported by the Excel CVE-2017-0199 report. Correct answers A (T1071.001 - HTTP GET requests evidenced), C (T1132 - Base64/Base62 encoded HTTP requests evidenced), D (T1573 - HTTPS traffic evidenced), and F (T1071.004 - DNS queries evidenced) are all directly supported. Options B (T1486 - no encryption for impact) and E (T1041 - no explicit exfiltration over C2 channel evidence beyond generic C2 contact) are correctly excluded. This question is very similar to question_index 9 which covers the same report and same topic. However, since both are in the generated exam (not benchmark), this is not a plagiarism issue against the benchmark. The question is factually correct and original relative to the benchmark.

The question asks which behaviors provide explicit evidence of Defense Evasion vs. Discovery/Execution in a Fragtor/um_unhooking sample. Correct answers A (software packing + high-entropy sections) and D (timestomping + hidden window) are directly evidenced. Options B (system version queries - Discovery), C (Windows service creation - Persistence), and E (rpcrt4.dll/GetProcAddress - generic execution) are correctly excluded. This question is very similar to question_index 11 which covers the same report and same topic with nearly identical options and answers. This constitutes internal duplication within the generated exam, but since the benchmark check is against the benchmark questions (not other generated questions), and the question is factually correct, it passes the benchmark plagiarism check. However, the near-identical nature to Q11 is worth noting.

The question asks to identify sandbox/VM evasion behaviors vs. generic discovery in a Trojan.Generic/um_unhooking sample. Correct answers A (CPUID-based anti-VM trick - directly evidenced by 'The input sample contains a known anti-VM trick' malicious signature with 'VMware trick') and C (RDTSCP timing instructions - evidenced by 'The input sample possibly contains the RDTSCP instruction') are directly supported. Options B, D, E, F are correctly identified as generic discovery. The question is original relative to the benchmark and technically sound. Note: This is very similar to question_index 12 but covers a different report (different SHA256, different family context). The question is factually correct.

The question asks to identify Inhibit System Recovery behaviors vs. generic discovery in a HydraCrypt ransomware report. Correct answers A (vssadmin delete shadows), C (wbadmin delete catalog), and E (bcdedit recoveryenabled no) are all directly evidenced by the process command lines. Options B (OS version queries - Discovery) and D (Windows installation language - Discovery) are correctly excluded. Note: This is very similar to question_index 13 which covers the same report. However, the question is factually correct and original relative to the benchmark.

The question asks which ATT&CK techniques are explicitly supported by the Trojan.Regrun/killers report. Correct answers A (T1055 Process Injection), C (T1036.005 Match Legitimate Name or Location), E (T1113 Screen Capture), and G (T1027 Obfuscated Files or Information) are all directly supported by the report. Options D (T1003 Credential Dumping), F (T1566.001 Spearphishing), and H (T1486) are correctly excluded. Note: This is very similar to question_index 14 which covers the same report with nearly identical options and answers. However, the question is factually correct and original relative to the benchmark.

The question asks which behaviors provide explicit evidence of process injection vs. dynamic library loading in a Safengine-packed um_unhooking sample. Correct answers B (writes data to remote process + creates thread - evidenced by 'Writes data to a remote process' malicious signature) and E (multiple MITRE ATT&CK entries for DLL Injection, Process Hollowing, Process Doppelganging - evidenced in MITRE mapping) are directly supported. Options A, C, D are correctly excluded. The question is original relative to the benchmark and technically sound. Note: Similar in structure to question_index 15 but covers a different report.
